title: The Weight of Unspoken Words: Anger at Censorship and Quiet Erasure
dek: When your voice is systematically silenced, the body absorbs the impact. The quiet erasure of your truth doesn't disappear; it settles into the fascia, tightening the jaw and restricting the breath. This is how we learn to read the somatic map of censorship and reclaim the space we were told to vacate.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Body Tightens When Your Voice Is Taken


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T01:52:42.969Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

You know the feeling before you even realize what’s happening: the slow, insidious squeeze in your throat, as if a thin band of steel coils itself around your larynx, tightening with every breath you try to pull in. It’s not just metaphorical - this is the masseter muscle clenching so hard you can taste the faint copper tang of your own tension on your tongue. Your jaw locks, the fascia between your cheekbones and ears taut like a drawn bowstring. You want to speak, to shout, to rage, but the breath barely makes it past your diaphragm, which is caught in a shallow, jittery rhythm. That breath that should carry your words is stolen, clipped, left gasping in the ribcage’s cage.

This is the physical map of being silenced.

The throat, a soft highway for your voice, becomes a bottleneck. The levator scapulae muscles at the back of your neck stiffen as if bracing for impact, pulling your head slightly forward, down, away from the world. You feel smaller, folded into yourself. The air tastes stale in your mouth, and the urge to swallow is constant - like your body is trying to reset, to clear the blockage that’s not just in your throat but in your very presence.

And yet, there is no loud, violent erasure here. No dramatic upheaval. Instead, this is quiet erasure: a slow, unseen siphoning of your right to exist fully, to be heard, to claim space. It’s the body’s way of registering that your voice is being sidelined, dismissed, or deliberately muted. This kind of erasure isn’t the clanging of a slammed door but the barely perceptible soft click of a lock sliding into place.

Quiet erasure is the background noise of your anger: simmering beneath the surface, a pressure building in the trapezius and psoas, tightening your shoulders and gut, stealing your grounding. It’s a subtle violence, often overlooked because it wears the disguise of “politeness” or “order.” But your body remembers. The muscles remember. The fascia remembers. This is not pathology. This is biology responding to injustice.

Teachers like Karla McLaren have shown us that anger is a messenger, not a villain. When your voice is stolen, your body doesn’t just sit still - it reacts. The masseter tightens, the diaphragm flattens, and your breath becomes a hesitant whisper. This is your body calling out, saying, “I am here. I matter. I will not be erased, even if my words are.”

The herbs that support this kind of tension - wild lettuce for its gentle muscle relaxation, skullcap to soothe the nervous system, and valerian for the restless breath - can help ease the physical hold of being silenced. But the first step is to notice the tightening, to feel the throat like a clenched fist, and to honor the anger that stirs beneath the quiet. Because when they quiet you, your body still speaks.

## The Anatomy of Censorship: How the Body Stores the Words We Swallow

You don’t just hold anger in your head - the body keeps the score, as Bessel van der Kolk reminds us, in the sinews and fascia, in the slow, unyielding contracture of muscle. When words are censored, when your truth is silenced or erased, it’s not just a mental censorship; it’s embodied. This is no metaphor. Your body registers the refusal to speak, to be heard, as a tightening, a gripping, a weight.

Start with the psoas. That deep core muscle, often called the "muscle of the soul," stretches from the lumbar spine, threading through your pelvis, attaching to the femur. When you swallow your fury - when you clamp down on what wants to roar - the psoas tightens. It holds you in a fetal curl of containment, a primal brace against the chaos of expression. You might notice this as a dull ache low in your back around 2 a.m., when the house is quiet and your mind starts to spin with all the things you wanted to say but didn’t. It’s the psoas whispering its protest, a muscle remembering that your voice was never allowed to fully emerge.

Then there’s the diaphragm, that dome-shaped muscle beneath your lungs, the boundary between your chest and belly. It’s the breath’s gatekeeper, the subtle engine of your voice. When anger is censored, the diaphragm clamps down, restricting your breath, like a gate slammed shut. Your inhales become shallow, your exhales clipped. You might feel a pressure just below your sternum, a tightness that smells faintly of stale air and unfinished sentences. This constriction isn’t just physical; it’s the body’s way of holding back the fire of your fury, the heat of your indignation that demands release.

The physical weight of unexpressed anger is not hypothetical. It presses on your trapezius and levator scapulae, those muscles around your shoulders and neck, pulling your head forward, as if trying to hide from the world that refuses to listen. The jaw clenches - the masseter spasms - because your body is rehearsing the words you never speak. You might wake with a sore jaw, the taste of copper in your mouth, the remnants of a silent scream trapped in the masseter’s memory.

In Traditional Chinese Medicine, this kind of repression disrupts the flow of qi in the Liver meridian, which governs anger. The body’s energy stagnates, turning sharp edges into knots and hardening the fascia like armor. Herbal allies like Bupleurum (Chai Hu) can help soothe this stagnation, calming the Liver’s fury and loosening the grip on the diaphragm and psoas, but the first step is recognizing that your body is not failing you. It is carrying your censored words, your muted rebellion, with incredible resilience.

You can feel this anatomy of censorship as a lived experience - the tight coil in the psoas, the shallow catch of the diaphragm, the grinding tension in the jaw. This is not pathology, this is biology. Your body is telling you a story about what you’ve been forced to swallow, and it aches to be read.

## The Herbal Ally: Rooting Your Fury When Your Voice Is Muffled

It’s three in the morning. Your jaw  -  the masseter muscle  -  clenches so tightly you can taste the tension, bitter and metallic, like old pennies. The back of your neck, the levator scapulae, knots into a tight fist that refuses to let go. This is the body’s way of speaking before your mouth gets a chance. When your fury rises against the quiet erasure of your voice, when censorship clamps down like a cold hand around your throat, you need allies that meet you in that raw, tremulous body of yours. Herbs are not sentimental; they are precise allies, rooted in the earthy intelligence of the plant kingdom and time-tested by those who knew how to listen. 

Wood Betony (Stachys officinalis) is one of these allies. It’s known as the "herb of the mind" for a reason that goes far beyond folklore. Your brain, that ceaseless chatterbox, is often the first to rebel when your words are dismissed or silenced. Wood Betony is like a balm for the fascia around the temples and the thick band of tissue encasing the occipital ridge, where tension accumulates when you’re holding back your truth. This herb eases not only the sharp pangs around the trigeminal nerve but also the clenching of the diaphragm  -  the very muscle that contracts in frustration when you feel unheard. When you sip a tea of Wood Betony, you invite a gentle loosening, a softening that doesn’t ask you to mute your anger, but rather to embody it with clarity and calm. Here’s a reliable source for Wood Betony [Solaray Total Calm Advanced](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B000Z1I5EC?tag=spankyspinola-20).

Then there’s Blue Vervain (Verbena hastata), a nervous system tonic that feels like a cool breeze across the trapezius and down the spine, soothing the fight-or-flight that surges when you’re pushed to the margins. Blue Vervain is a bitter herb, and bitterness in TCM (Traditional Chinese Medicine) relates to the Heart and Small Intestine meridians  -  organs deeply tied to joy and communication. When you’re angry about being erased, it’s not just your voice that suffers but your capacity to feel seen and valued. Blue Vervain helps untangle the knots in your sympathetic nervous system, the one that tightens your throat and spikes your heartbeat. Taking it as a tincture or infusion can ground the restless energy in your psoas muscle  -  that deep core muscle that holds your fight or flight in place, often without your awareness. If you want to meet this herb on your own terms, here’s a good place to find it: [Host Defense Lion's Mane Powder](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07TJ63MH3?tag=spankyspinola-20).

These herbs work with your biology, not against it. They don’t demand you sit quietly or "just breathe," which can often feel like a command to erase yourself further. Instead, they offer a way to tend the living, vibrating body that refuses to be silenced. The jaw remembers every harsh word withheld; the diaphragm knows the weight of every deep, stifled breath. Wood Betony and Blue Vervain don’t erase that memory; they support the nervous system to carry it without breaking. As you move through the slow hours of the night or the bright glare of daylight, these herbs can be a tactile reminder that your anger is not a failure but a fierce, necessary signal. And tending that signal  -  with the right allies  -  is the first step toward reclaiming your voice.

## Generational Silence: The Quiet Erasure Passed Down

You feel it first in the hollow behind your sternum, that slow, sinking weight when you try to speak a truth that’s been folded away, tucked under layers of unspoken rules and invisible walls. The silence isn’t just absence; it’s a body memory, a muscle learned tight in the masseter, a catch in the diaphragm. Resmaa Menakem calls this the legacy of generational trauma, carried not just in stories but in the very fascia and fibers of your body. When you hold anger at censorship and quiet erasure, you’re not only wrestling with external forces but a lineage of muted voices that have been passed down through your blood and breath.

Menakem’s somatic abolitionism teaches us that trauma isn’t just a psychological imprint; it’s an embodied inheritance. Your levator scapulae might rise, knotting around your neck, in moments when speaking up feels dangerous or forbidden. This is not pathology, this is biology. The body remembers the times your ancestors swallowed their fury, not because they lacked fire, but because survival depended on silence. Their jaw muscles clenched so tightly that the memory of that clenched masseter is lodged in your own flesh, a somatic echo of erasure.

This silence is not neutral. It is a form of censorship that seeps into your cells before it hits your ears. You learn early to smooth your voice, to soften the edges of your anger like a balm - maybe something bitter and strong like dried skullcap root steeped for hours, the kind of tea Karla McLaren might recommend to soothe the nervous system without dulling the flame. But that soothing can become a shackle when it teaches you to mute your fire instead of listening to what it wants to say.

The generational passing down of quiet erasure is a slow, insidious weaving of “don’t make waves,” “better to keep peace,” “your anger is dangerous.” These phrases aren’t just words; they’re instructions written into the connective tissue around your ribs, the fascia that links your psoas to your lower back, tightening you into a shape that is less threatening, more palatable. The body knows these instructions well, learned in the midnight hours when the house is still and the only sound is the faint rattle of your breath, shallow and guarded.

Menakem’s work insists that abolitionism is not only about external systems of oppression but the internal systems of holding trauma and silence in the body. To unlearn the inheritance of quiet erasure, you must first feel the body’s resistance - not as failure, but as a messenger. The trap isn’t just the censorship outside you; it’s also the censorship inside, the self-policing that clamps down on your own fierce voice before it has a chance to roar.

You don’t have to wait for permission to begin unraveling this silence. The body is ready to teach you if you’re willing to listen to the subtle shift in your trapezius, the softening of the nape muscles, the release of tension that smells faintly of old smoke or damp earth. This is the work of reclaiming your anger - not as a problem to hide, but as an inheritance to honor and transform. Quiet erasure is a ghost in your cells, but it can be exorcised, muscle by muscle, breath by breath.

## The Somatic Map of Anger: Your Body’s Geography of Erasure

When your voice is silenced, when the world clips your edges and folds you into silence, your body becomes the first and fiercest witness. This is not metaphor. You can feel the heat pooling in your chest like molten iron, the way the sternum seems to press outward against invisible walls. The muscles around your heart - your pectoralis major and minor - tighten as if bracing against an unspoken weight. This is the body’s way of saying, *I am here. I matter*. The heat isn’t just metaphorical fury; it’s real, a biochemical blaze fueled by adrenaline and cortisol coursing through your blood, setting your fascia on edge.

Look beneath that fire, into the muscles that cradle your neck, especially the levator scapulae. This muscle, running from the upper cervical vertebrae down to the top of your shoulder blades, stiffens like a wired trap. You might notice the tension there when you tilt your head slightly, a subtle resistance that tightens your trapezius and threads down your spine. This is the body's silent scream, the way anger at erasure lodges in the muscles that hold your head high - because your head *deserves* to be held high.

Karla McLaren, who has spent decades mapping the emotional landscape with precision and tenderness, teaches us that anger is not a failure or a flaw - it is a boundary-restoring emotion. When you are erased, censored, or pushed into invisibility, anger is the body’s natural response to reclaim the space you have been denied. It is the diaphragm’s sharp intake of breath, the way your masseter clenches in tight, protective readiness. The jaw remembers every time you were told to “be quiet,” every time your words melted into the hush. That clenched masseter holds the memory of all those silences, and when anger rises, it demands release.

Your body’s response to this kind of erasure isn’t just about fight or flight; it’s about *reasserting presence* - a somatic declaration that you *exist*. The psoas muscle, deep in your core, often tightens in fear or frustration, pulling your pelvis forward as if preparing you to stand your ground or flee. Yet, in the context of anger at censorship, it can also feel like a coil of energy waiting to unspool, ready to claim space that was denied.

This is not pathology, this is biology. Your body is wired to protect your boundaries. When those boundaries are violated by erasure, your body responds with heat, tension, and breath that quickens - not because you are broken, but because your nervous system is doing its job. It is sounding the alarm. It is telling you that your voice matters, that your presence is non-negotiable.

To map this anger is to honor it. It is to trace the lines of tension from your jaw to your shoulders, from your chest to your core, and to say, *I feel you. I hear you.* It is to recognize that the heat in your chest is not a fire to be extinguished, but a flame to be tended. It is the fuel for your voice, the energy that will carry your words out of the silence and into the world.

## Embodied Practice: Releasing the Swallowed Words

When the quiet erasure has settled into your bones, when the censorship has become a physical weight pressing down on your chest, you need a practice that speaks directly to the body. This is not about thinking your way out of anger; it is about feeling your way through it. It is about giving the body permission to release the words it has swallowed, to untangle the knots in the fascia, and to reclaim the space that was taken from you.

Begin by finding a quiet space where you won't be disturbed. Sit or stand comfortably, feeling the ground beneath your feet. Notice the points of contact between your body and the earth. This is your foundation, your anchor in the storm of anger. Take a slow, deep breath in through your nose, feeling the air fill your lungs and expand your ribcage. As you exhale, let out a soft sigh, releasing any immediate tension in your shoulders and neck.

Now, bring your attention to your jaw. The masseter muscle is often the first to clench when we are silenced, holding the memory of unspoken words. Gently place your fingertips on your jawline, just below your ears. Apply a light, steady pressure, massaging the muscle in small circles. Notice any tenderness or resistance. As you massage, allow your jaw to drop slightly, creating space between your upper and lower teeth. Breathe into this space, imagining the tension melting away with each exhale.

Next, move your focus to your throat. This is the gateway of your voice, the place where words are formed and released. Place one hand gently over your throat, feeling the warmth of your palm against your skin. Take a deep breath in, and as you exhale, make a low, humming sound. Feel the vibration in your vocal cords, the gentle resonance in your chest. This is not about making a loud noise; it is about feeling the physical sensation of your voice, reminding your body that it has the power to speak.

Finally, bring your attention to your diaphragm, the muscle that controls your breath. Place both hands on your belly, just below your ribcage. Take a slow, deep breath in, feeling your belly expand into your hands. As you exhale, gently press your hands inward, helping to empty your lungs completely. Repeat this deep, diaphragmatic breathing for a few minutes, allowing your breath to become slow and steady. This is the breath of presence, the breath that grounds you in your body and reminds you that you are here, you are alive, and your voice matters.

## Reclaiming the Voice: The Physical Act of Speaking Up

The act of speaking up after being silenced is not just a mental decision; it is a physical event. It is the culmination of the body's resistance against quiet erasure, the moment when the tension in the masseter and the constriction in the diaphragm are finally released. When you open your mouth to speak your truth, you are not just forming words; you are reclaiming your physical space in the world.

You feel it first in the deep intake of breath, the way the diaphragm expands, pushing against the resistance of the psoas and the intercostal muscles. This is the breath of courage, the breath that fuels the fire of your voice. As the air rushes into your lungs, you might feel a sudden rush of heat in your chest, a surge of adrenaline that sharpens your focus and quickens your pulse. This is your body preparing for action, marshaling its resources to support your voice.

Then comes the vibration. As the air passes through your vocal cords, you feel the physical resonance of your words. It starts as a low hum in your throat, a vibration that spreads outward, filling your chest and echoing in your skull. This is the sound of your presence, the undeniable proof that you exist and that you have something to say. The vibration is a physical force, a wave of energy that pushes back against the silence and demands to be heard.

The act of speaking up also changes your posture. The levator scapulae and the trapezius, which may have been pulling your head forward in a posture of submission, suddenly release. Your spine lengthens, your shoulders drop, and your head lifts. You are no longer folding into yourself; you are expanding, taking up space, asserting your right to be seen and heard. This physical expansion is a powerful antidote to the quiet erasure that sought to diminish you.

Reclaiming your voice is a somatic victory. It is the body's way of saying, *I will not be erased.* It is a physical declaration of your worth, a testament to the resilience of your spirit and the power of your anger. When you speak your truth, you are not just breaking the silence; you are healing the physical wounds of censorship, one word, one breath, one vibration at a time.

## FAQ: Understanding the Somatic Impact of Censorship

**Q: Why does my jaw hurt when I feel like I can't speak my mind?**

**A:** The jaw, specifically the masseter muscle, is deeply connected to our ability to express ourselves. When you are silenced or feel unable to speak your truth, the body often responds by clenching the jaw, as if physically holding back the words. This chronic tension can lead to pain, stiffness, and even temporomandibular joint (TMJ) issues. It is the body's way of storing the unexpressed anger and frustration of being censored.

**Q: Can suppressed anger really cause physical symptoms like shallow breathing?**

**A:** Yes, absolutely. The diaphragm, the primary muscle of respiration, is highly sensitive to emotional stress. When you suppress anger, the diaphragm often tightens, restricting your breath and leading to shallow, chest-breathing patterns. This is a physical manifestation of the body's attempt to contain the intense energy of anger. Over time, this restricted breathing can contribute to feelings of anxiety, fatigue, and a sense of being disconnected from your body.

**Q: How can herbs help with the physical symptoms of quiet erasure?**

**A:** Herbs can be powerful allies in supporting the nervous system and releasing physical tension. Nervine herbs, such as Wood Betony and Blue Vervain, help to calm the nervous system, reduce muscle spasms, and ease the physical grip of suppressed anger. They do not erase the anger, but rather support the body in processing and releasing it, allowing you to move from a state of chronic tension to one of grounded presence.

**Q: Is it normal to feel exhausted after finally speaking up about something that was silenced?**

**A:** Yes, it is very normal. Holding back your truth requires a tremendous amount of physical and emotional energy. When you finally speak up, the body releases the chronic tension it has been holding, which can lead to a sudden drop in adrenaline and cortisol levels. This release can leave you feeling physically and emotionally drained. It is important to rest and support your body during this time, allowing it to recover from the exertion of reclaiming your voice.
